run spdk in multi-process mode as secondary for daos_io_server
Currently running multiple _daos_io_server_s on same node is not supported, because each _daos_io_server_ will call _spdk_env_init()_ in ‘primary’ mode and reserve hugepages. In that situation, the first started _daos_io_server_ will use up all the hugepages, and other _daos_io_server_s will fail to get any available hugepages.

To support multiple _daos_io_server_s on same node (and in order to perform discovery while running _daos_io_servers_), SPDK global initialization should be performed in _daos_server_ (including _spdk_env_init()_), configuring multi-process mode so that _daos_server_ runs in ‘primary’ mode and the _daos_io_server_s run in ‘secondary’ mode.

Provide system-level metrics aggregation
DAOS-4449 tracks the design and implementation of a solution for getting ioserver statistics into the control plane server on each storage node, but it may not be a complete solution.

Performing system-wide queries of metrics would be prohibitively expensive at scale if the query agent had to fan out to each storage node to query them individually and then aggregate in real-time.

It would be ideal if our tooling were able to integrate with a system-level metrics store to perform system-wide metrics queries without a fanout. This metrics store might be something we provide ourselves (e.g. Prometheus, etc), or might be provided by the system integrator (e.g. LDMS, etc).

Conditional modification inside distributed transaction
The distributed transaction caches modifications on client before being committed. Single conditional modification is equal to atomic "fetch" + "update/punch". If such conditional operation is inside distributed transaction, if we still cache non-committed modifications, then we will not handle "fetch" that is the first part of the conditional modification. So the result for the left part "update/punch" may be different from the case of being executed on the server. That may further misguide the application to make take wrong more actions in subsequent operations.

Accurate mtime
Many tools turns out to rely on an accurate mtime. This includes compilers as well as copy tools/data mover (mfu, rsync, ...). We should thus consider a way to report an accurate mtime in dfs. On the other hand, we really don't want to impact the performance by doing extra RPC "just" for mtime.

The proposal is thus to calculate mtime on the fly in stat(2) by modifying the key query used today to calculate the file size to report the highest epoch allocated to an extent. We can then take the highest value across all the shards/groups, convert it to a timestamp and report it as mtime.

Improve error reporting and diagnostics capabilities
Recently, on the Endeavour cluster, the system got into some kind of state where the MS was running (i.e. dmg system query showed the membership, dmg pool list --no-query showed the pools), but most of the ranks were in Excluded or Errored states. We still don't know exactly what happened -- my suspicion is that the AP was started after the rest of the ranks and a bug (DAOS-8304; fixed in TB6) prevented them from retrying their Join operations.

Attempts to stop/start the entire system would result in RPC timeouts. Attempts to stop/start individual ranks would also result in RPC timeouts. The State and Reason fields in the system query output did not change, however, so there was no way to tell what was wrong with the system without inspecting the server logs. However, as the servers were configured to run with control_log_mask: ERROR, the logs didn't contain any details either.

We can learn from this experience and work to make the control plane a more useful tool in diagnosing problems.

Control Plane Error Handling Updates
Creating this as a placeholder for further discussion.

Currently, the error handling in the various control plane components is fairly ad-hoc and not particularly cohesive. In some cases there are error strings assigned to variables which are used to compose full error messages. In other cases error strings are supplied via errors.Wrap() or errors.WithMessage() in order to denote where the error occurred (e.g. "loading config").

These informal styles of handling errors are fine for now while the project is in a state of rapid development, but we should be thinking long-term. Error handling is a critical component of RAS in a large-scale system, and getting it right will be crucial to the success of the project.

[Reliability, Availability, Serviceability|https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reliability,_availability_and_serviceability]:
 * Reliability: Our software should be trusted to detect error conditions and handle them appropriately. The worst thing that could happen, especially at scale, would be for our software to miss or suppress errors which result in data loss or undefined and difficult to diagnose behavior.
 * Availability: Our software should be written defensively to handle errors and unexpected conditions as gracefully as possible. In this context, "graceful" handling implies careful consideration of whether or not an error or unexpected condition is truly fatal or could be dealt with in a way which keeps the system running in a defined state. Add a test for creating two value types under the same akey
Environment:

1 Server/ 1 Client

Purpose:
 * Verify that akey can only support one type of value which is set on first update.

Description:

Case 1:
 * Create Pool
 * insert dkey1, akey1, Single value
 * insert dkey1, akey1, array value (should return an error)
 * Expected Result: -DER_NO_PERM

Case 2:
 * Create Pool
 * insert dkey1, akey1, Single value
 * punch dkey1, akey1
 * insert dkey1, akey1, array value
 * Expected Result: Either Pass or return -DER_NO_PERM

 Case 3:
 * Create Pool
 * insert dkey1, akey1, Single value
 * punch dkey1, akey1
 * trigger aggregation
 * insert dkey1, akey1, array value
 * Expected Result: Either Pass or return -DER_NO_PERM

 

Original description:
 a simple test to address this new restriction:

Note that an akey can only support one type of value which is set on the first update. Any subsequent attempts to write a different type will fail with -DER_NO_PERM. Additionally, the type persists even after the akey is punched and can only be changed after the key is removed by aggregation.

Update ipmctl to v02.00.00.3820 to prevent to avoid failure in setting goal
When running DAOS and through ipmctl which is called at runtime, setting PMem goal fails if 02.00.00.3809-3816 version of ipmctl is installed and _dmg storage prepare_ command is run to set persistent memory modules into PppDirect mode for use with DAOS. The failure is due to a corruption of the PCD on the DIMMs and results in system POST memory checks failing with fatal error.

ipmctl was fixed in [this commit|https://github.com/intel/ipmctl/commit/9e3898cb15fa9eed3ef3e9de4488be1681d53ff4] released in version [v02.00.00.3820|https://github.com/intel/ipmctl/releases/tag/v02.00.00.3820]

That situation then requires recovery of persistent memory modules.

In normal operating conditions the problem situation is unlikely to arise during installation due to reasons explained in this ticket's comments but [https://github.com/daos-stack/daos/pull/3878] has been created to restrict, during RPM install, the version of ipmctl being installed for use by DAOS at runtime. That should prevent existing installed ipmctl packages of affected version from causing issues with new DAOS installations. Existing installations can prevent any issues by upgrading ipmctl to the latest available OS distro version.

Resolution, as described in PR commit message:

 
{code:java}
Restrict ipmctl version installed through RPM as daos_server runtime
dependency to avoid 02.00.00.3809-3816 which causes corruption of PCD
data when setting PMem configuration goal to AppDirect mode in BIOS
during
